title: The quiet violence of "playing it safe" with your money.
subtitle: Most people don't lose money on bad investments. They lose it on the good ones they were too afraid to make.
author: Bhartendra Singh
body_html:
There's a particular kind of investor I keep meeting. Disciplined. Smart. Risk-averse in the way that gets praised at dinner parties. They have a savings account, an FD, maybe an LIC policy from 2007, and a sense of quiet pride that they've never "gambled" with the markets.

They are also, almost without exception, getting poorer every year. They just don't know it yet.

The maths nobody wants to do

Inflation in India has averaged roughly 6% over the last decade. Your FD pays you 7%. After tax, you take home maybe 5%. That means every year, your "safe" money is losing about 1% of its real value. Over thirty years, that's a third of your purchasing power. Gone, not because you spent it, but because you refused to let it work.

This is the part the financial industry rarely says out loud. The most expensive decision most Indians make is not buying the wrong stock. It's refusing to buy any stock at all.

"Playing it safe" is not a strategy. It's a story we tell ourselves to justify not thinking about something that scares us.

The cost of waiting one decade

Consider two people. Priya starts investing ₹10,000 a month at age 25. Rajesh starts at 35. Same amount, same fund, same 12% average return. By the time they're both 55, Priya has roughly ₹3.5 crores. Rajesh has roughly ₹1 crore. Same effort. Same discipline. The only variable was when fear stopped being the boss.

The first decade is the most expensive one to skip. The second one is harder to recover from than the first one was to begin.

Why it feels safe

Volatility looks like risk. It isn't. Loss of purchasing power is risk. The market drops 30% and recovers in 18 months, that's an inconvenience. Your money sits in an FD for 30 years compounding at 1% real, that's a quiet, permanent erasure of your future. One is loud. The other is loud only at the end, when there's nothing left to do.

If you're reading this and thinking but I have responsibilities, I can't take risks, I'd gently suggest that responsibility is precisely why you can't afford not to.
excerpt: There's a particular kind of investor I keep meeting. Disciplined. Smart. Risk-averse in the way that gets praised at dinner parties. They have a savings account, an FD, maybe an LIC policy from 2007,…

title: Why your SIP's underperformance is not your fund manager's fault.
subtitle: The maths of investor behaviour beats fund selection, most of the time.
author: Bhartendra Singh
body_html:
Open any mutual fund factsheet and you'll see two numbers: the fund's return, and the investor's return. The investor's return is almost always lower. Sometimes by a little. Often by a lot.

This isn't because the funds are lying. It's because investors enter at the wrong time, exit at the wrong time, and pause SIPs exactly when they should be doubling down.

The 7% gap

Across the last 15 years, the average Indian equity mutual fund has delivered ~13% CAGR. The average investor in those funds has earned ~7%. That 6-point gap isn't fees. It isn't tax. It's behaviour.

What to do about it

Three things, in order of impact:

  • Automate everything. If your SIP requires a monthly decision, you'll eventually make the wrong one.
  • Never check NAV during a fall. Looking is the first step toward selling.
  • Pre-commit your response to a crash. Write down, today, what you'll do if your portfolio drops 30%. That note is worth more than any fund selection.

The fund manager's job is to deliver the fund's return. Yours is to actually receive it.
excerpt: Open any mutual fund factsheet and you'll see two numbers: the fund's return, and the investor's return. The investor's return is almost always lower. Sometimes by a little. Often by a lot.

title: The new tax regime, decoded for people who'd rather not read 47 pages.
subtitle: Eleven minutes to know what most CAs spend hours explaining.
author: Bhartendra Singh
body_html:
The new tax regime is simpler. That's its only real selling point, and for most people, that's enough.

The actual decision

Old regime: lower headline rates if you claim deductions. New regime: lower flat rates with almost no deductions allowed.

If your annual deductions are under ₹3 lakh, the new regime usually wins. Above that, do the math both ways.

Who benefits

  • Salaried with no HRA, no home loan, no 80C investments beyond EPF, new regime, easy win
  • Home loan + 80C maxed + HRA, old regime, still better
  • Senior citizens with medical premiums, usually old, by a bit

The thing nobody mentions

The new regime is structurally designed to win over time. Each budget tweaks slabs upward; deductions get harder. If you're under 40 and starting your career, defaulting to new and forgetting about it is rarely the wrong choice.
excerpt: The new tax regime is simpler. That's its only real selling point, and for most people, that's enough.
